Yes, I think that summary is basically what I had in mind.

Note that the user can wait until after applying any transformations or convolving by a galaxy before calling `setupInterpolation`, which would then let them use the interpolation machinery on the final object.

In fact, if you don't want to try to handle the transformations of objects that have the interpolation set up, you could just let them revert back to a simple ChromaticObject without interpolation and emit a warning that the previous setupInterpolation call doesn't apply anymore.  (Or maybe only do this if the transformation has a wavelength dependence, since the achromatic ones are handleable.)  Similarly if they convolve by another chromatic object.

> Note that the user can wait until after applying any transformations or convolving by a galaxy before 
> calling setupInterpolation, which would then let them use the interpolation machinery on the final 
> object.

This requires that subclasses like ```ChromaticOpticalPSF``` or whatever other subclasses have their ```evaluateAtWavelength``` method set up to apply the transformations, right?  This way the stored images will have the transformation already applied. (In fact, this must be a requirement for ChromaticObject in general.)

That sounds neat in principle but I think it is not what we would want in many cases.  For example, imagine we have a ```ChromaticAtmosphere``` that we want to allow to have a mildly varying size and shape for each position on the sky, with the variation being implemented using ```shear``` and ```dilate``` with position-dependent arguments.  In the scenario you describe, the user would have to set up the interpolation machinery separately at each location, whereas if we use some trickery to allow the transformation (at least achromatic ones) on the final InterpolatedImage, the user could set up the interpolation machinery just once for the original object, and then use it for all the slightly different PSFs at each position.

I guess we could just advise the user of these tradeoffs, i.e., that
```
foo = galsim.ChromaticAtmosphere(...)
foo = foo.shear(...)
foo.setupInterpolation()
im = foo.drawImage(...)
```
is doing something different from
```
foo = galsim.ChromaticAtmosphere(...)
foo.setupInterpolation()
foo = foo.shear(...)
im = foo.drawImage(...)
```
and they should think about what makes sense for their use case.

In the first case, we want ```evaluateAtWavelength``` to carry out all the transformations before storing the images, whereas in the second case, we want ```drawImage``` to carry out the transformations at the end on the final InterpolatedImage right before drawing.   Though I guess that we could deal with that like this:

* In the first case, ```evaluateAtWavelength``` could do the transformation and set some flag like self.transformed=True (where it is set to False in the ChromaticObject constructor).  Then after interpolation is set up, it's going to use the custom interpolation version of drawImage which doesn't call ```evaluateAtWavelength```, but rather ```_imageAtWavelength```, where the stored images already have the transformation. The routine will check for self.transformed=True, and that tells it to not use ```self._A``` to transform the final InterpolatedImage.

* In the second case, the custom interpolation version of drawImage will find ```self.transformed=False```, and that is its signal that it should transform using the achromatic ```_A``` matrix after making the InterpolatedImage.  (If ```_A``` is chromatic then it would, as you say, revert to a standard ChromaticObject and emit a warning.)

 1. The purpose of `shell_plus` is to automatically import models and some other objects from Django in order to not to import them by hand when someone want to run the shell. This works even with plain Python shell.
 2. Default `shell` command from Django also automatically use ipython if available.
 3. Non of the extra shells are required for `shell_plus` to work (this is probably as 1. but stated differently). Other management commands needs some packages in order to work at all.

> Do we have to submit all patches using just the email that signed the CLA? Seems kind of a turn off.. hope we can whitelist several emails / GH users under the CLA.

Any e-mail that is set as the "author" of a commit in a PR needs to have signed the CLA (otherwise, we don't necessarily know if those commits are yours...). So if you author commits as a@a.com and b@b.com and send a single pull request, both of those e-mails must have signed the CLA -- it's OK to have multiple in a single PR though.

If you have a ton of people and want to do a blanket signature, there's a way to sign "for the company" ([here](https://cla.developers.google.com/clas/new?kind=KIND_CORPORATE&domain=DOMAIN_GOOGLE)) and you just maintain a Google Group with all the e-mails that are "authorized".

This is a good feature request. I agree that such a change would improve usability.

I see two ways to address this:
1. To allow either `Table` or `TableReference` for operations such as `client.load_table_from_file()`. Likewise for `Dataset` and `DatasetReference`.
2. Add a helper method to get the `TableReference` from a `Table` object. Likewise for `Dataset` and `DatasetReference`.

In the meantime, the workaround is to create a [`TableReference`](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html#google.cloud.bigquery.table.TableReference) manually from a `Table`.

```
dataset_ref = DatasetReference(table.project, table.dataset_id)
table_ref = TableReference(dataset_ref, table.table_id)
```

My preference would be for (2). Something like the following code:

```
table_ref = table.reference
```
